title: Connecting Units 1-4: where do the concepts build on each other?
body:
As we head into the final unit I'm trying to build a coherent picture of how the course hangs together. A few connections I can see:

- The efficiency concern from Unit 1 reappears in Unit 3 when evaluating approach complexity.
- The assumption analysis in Unit 4 seems to require the vocabulary from Unit 2 to be precise.

Are there connections I'm missing? I find it easier to retain material when I can see the structure rather than treating each unit as isolated.
author: student

comments.author: user-1
comments.body: The connection I noticed that isn't obvious at first: the invariant you need to maintain in Approach B (Unit 3) is essentially the same kind of constraint as the acyclicity requirement in Unit 1. In both cases the algorithm's correctness depends on a structural property of the input that you have to verify or guarantee externally. The lecture treats them as separate topics but the reasoning pattern is identical.

comments.author: student
comments.body: That's a really useful frame. Building on that — the stationarity assumption in Unit 4 is also a structural constraint on the input, just a temporal one rather than a graph-theoretic one. So all three units are really asking the same question: what does the input have to be for this to work?

comments.author: admin
comments.body: This is exactly the kind of synthesis I was hoping this thread would generate. The pattern you're both describing — algorithm correctness depending on a structural property of the input — is a genuine throughline. Unit 2 adds to it: the assumption there is about the independence structure of the features, which is again a claim about what the input looks like. What you're noticing is not coincidence.

comments.author: user-1
comments.body: If that pattern holds, then the question for the final assessment might be less about recalling each unit's content and more about: what structural property does this problem require, and how would you verify it holds? That feels like a more generalisable way to prepare.

comments.author: student
comments.body: Agreed. And it also gives a cleaner way to spot when an algorithm is being misapplied: check whether the structural property it depends on actually holds for the problem at hand.
